class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Managerial Accounting and Cost Concepts
learning_objectives: Distinguish managerial accounting from financial accounting
Classify costs as direct/indirect, variable/fixed, and product/period
readings: IMA Statement of Ethical Professional Practice

lecture_notes.introduction: While financial accounting reports to external users, managerial accounting provides internal decision makers with the cost and performance data they need to plan, control, and make informed decisions.
key_concepts: Financial vs. managerial accounting purposes and audiences
Direct materials, direct labor, manufacturing overhead
Variable, fixed, and mixed cost behaviors
Product costs vs. period costs
Cost objects and cost drivers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Cost classification exercise using a real manufacturing company's cost data

review.value: Costs that change in total proportionally with activity level but remain constant per unit
Costs that remain constant in total regardless of activity level but decrease per unit as volume increases

learning_objectives: Calculate break-even point in units and dollars

lecture_notes.introduction: CVP analysis is one of the most powerful tools for managers. It reveals how changes in costs, volume, and price affect profitability.
key_concepts: Contribution margin per unit and ratio
Break-even point: Q = Fixed Costs / CM per unit
Target profit analysis: Q = (FC + Target Profit) / CM
Margin of safety calculation
Operating leverage and its implications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a CVP model in Excel for a product launch decision, including sensitivity analysis

review.value: Revenue minus variable costs; the amount each unit contributes to covering fixed costs and generating profit
The volume at which total revenue equals total costs; contribution margin exactly covers fixed costs

learning_objectives: Apply job-order costing to custom production environments
Compare job-order and process costing systems

lecture_notes.introduction: Different production environments require different costing approaches. Job-order costing tracks costs by individual job, while process costing averages costs across identical units.
key_concepts: Job-order costing: job cost sheets and overhead allocation
Predetermined overhead rate = Estimated OH / Estimated activity
Over-applied and under-applied overhead
Process costing: equivalent units of production
Weighted-average vs. FIFO method
lecture_notes.demonstration: Complete job-order costing example for a custom furniture manufacturer

review.value: Calculated at beginning of period using estimates; applied to jobs based on actual activity during the period
In process costing, the number of complete units that could have been produced given the work done on partially complete units

learning_objectives: Prepare components of a master budget
Link sales budget to production, materials, and cash budgets
Evaluate flexible budgets for performance evaluation

lecture_notes.introduction: Budgeting translates strategic plans into financial terms. A master budget coordinates all departments toward common financial goals.
key_concepts: Sales budget as the starting point
Production budget: units to produce = sales + desired ending - beginning inventory
Direct materials, direct labor, and overhead budgets
Cash budget and financing needs
Flexible budgets: adjusting for actual volume
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete master budget for a quarter, starting from sales forecast through pro forma financial statements

review.value: An integrated set of budgets covering sales, production, costs, and financing for a planning period
A budget adjusted to actual activity level, enabling meaningful comparison of budgeted vs. actual costs

learning_objectives: Set standard costs for materials, labor, and overhead
Calculate and interpret price and quantity variances
Use variance analysis for performance evaluation

lecture_notes.introduction: Standard costing and variance analysis give managers a systematic way to identify and investigate deviations from expected performance.
key_concepts: Setting standard costs: ideal vs. practical standards
Materials price variance = (AQ)(AP - SP)
Materials quantity variance = (SP)(AQ - SQ)
Labor rate and efficiency variances
Overhead spending and volume variances
lecture_notes.demonstration: Complete variance analysis for a manufacturing department with investigation of significant variances

review.value: Favorable: actual cost less than standard; Unfavorable: actual cost exceeds standard. Neither is inherently good or bad without context.
Focus investigation on variances that exceed a materiality threshold, not every deviation

learning_objectives: Identify relevant and irrelevant costs for decisions
Apply differential analysis to make-or-buy decisions
Evaluate special order pricing and product line decisions

lecture_notes.introduction: Not all costs matter for every decision. Identifying which costs are relevant is the key skill that separates effective managers from those who make costly errors.
key_concepts: Relevant costs: future costs that differ between alternatives
Sunk costs are never relevant
Opportunity cost: value of the next best alternative foregone
Make-or-buy analysis
Special order decisions and constrained resource allocation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Multi-scenario decision analysis: outsourcing vs. in-house production with capacity constraints

review.value: A cost already incurred that cannot be recovered and should not influence future decisions
The benefit foregone by choosing one alternative over the next best option; always relevant for decisions

questions.question: A company sells widgets for $25 each. Variable costs are $15 per unit and fixed costs are $50,000 per month. Calculate the break-even point in units and the number of units needed to earn a target profit of $30,000.
questions.answer: Contribution margin = $25 - $15 = $10/unit. Break-even = $50,000 / $10 = 5,000 units. Target profit: ($50,000 + $30,000) / $10 = 8,000 units.

questions.question: Explain why a highly automated factory with mostly fixed costs has higher operating leverage than a labor-intensive factory. What are the risks and benefits of high operating leverage?
questions.answer: High operating leverage means a larger proportion of costs are fixed, so contribution margin per unit is high. Small increases in volume generate large profit increases (benefit), but small decreases in volume cause large profit drops (risk). The automated factory has high fixed costs from equipment but low variable costs, magnifying the profit impact of volume changes compared to the labor-intensive factory where costs adjust more with volume.

rubric.key: Explains risk of high leverage with example

questions.question: A company budgeted to use 2 pounds of material per unit at $5/pound. Actual production was 1,000 units using 2,200 pounds at $4.80/pound. Calculate the materials price variance and quantity variance, and state whether each is favorable or unfavorable.
questions.answer: Price variance = 2,200 x ($4.80 - $5.00) = 2,200 x (-$0.20) = -$440 Favorable. Quantity variance = $5.00 x (2,200 - 2,000) = $5.00 x 200 = $1,000 Unfavorable. Standard quantity = 1,000 units x 2 lbs = 2,000 lbs.

rubric.key: Correct price variance with favorable label
Correct quantity variance with unfavorable label

questions.question: Why is a flexible budget more useful than a static budget for evaluating a production manager's performance?
questions.answer: A static budget is based on one expected activity level, so comparing it to actual results confounds volume differences with cost control. A flexible budget adjusts expected costs to the actual volume achieved, isolating the manager's cost control performance from volume effects outside their control. For example, if actual volume is higher than planned, a static budget makes costs appear unfavorable simply due to more production, while a flexible budget shows whether costs per unit were controlled.

rubric.key: Identifies the problem with static budgets
Explains how flexible budgets isolate performance

major_assignments.title: Master Budget Development Project
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete master budget for a manufacturing company for one quarter, including sales, production, direct materials, direct labor, overhead, selling/administrative expense, and cash budgets, culminating in pro forma financial statements.
objectives: Apply budgeting techniques to realistic business data
Prepare pro forma financial statements from budget data
Analyze budget results and provide management recommendations
deliverables: Complete master budget workbook (Excel) with linked formulas
Pro forma income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement
Written analysis memo (3-5 pages) interpreting results
Sensitivity analysis showing impact of 10% sales variance

criteria: Cash budget shows correct financing needs
Pro forma statements are internally consistent
Complete and accurate master budget with linked schedules

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of cost concepts and analytical techniques

homework_problems.description: Weekly problem sets covering costing, budgeting, and variance analysis

major_assignments.description: Master budget project and decision analysis case

midterm_examination.description: Covers cost behavior, CVP analysis, and costing systems

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam emphasizing budgeting, variances, and decision making

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Students missing more than 3 classes without excuse will lose participation credit.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late submissions penalized 10% per day. Budget project has a hard deadline with no extensions.
course_policies.collaboration: Homework problems may be discussed in groups but must be submitted individually. The budget project is an individual assignment.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Use of solution manuals or prior semester submissions will result in a zero and academic integrity referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Proficiency in Excel or Google Sheets is expected. Calculator required for all exams and quizzes.
